headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext:
Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for standing by, and welcome to the Q4 2017 earnings call. [Operator Instructions] And as a reminder, this conference is being recorded.
I would now like to turn the conference over to our host, Mr. Dave Ricks. Please go ahead, sir.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext:
Good morning. Thank you for joining us for Eli Lilly and Company's fourth quarter 2017 earnings call. I'm Dave Ricks, Lilly's Chairman and CEO. Joining me on today's call are Josh Smiley, our CFO; Dr. Jan Lundberg, President of Lilly Research Labs; Enrique Conterno, President of Lilly Diabetes and Lilly USA; Dr. Sue Mahony, President of Lilly Oncology; Christi Shaw, President of Lilly Bio-Medicines; and Jeff Simmons, President of Our Elanco Animal Health business. We're also joined by Kristina Wright, Chris Ogden, Kevin Hearne, and Phil Johnson of the IR team.
During this call, we anticipate making projections and forward-looking statements based on our current expectations. Our actual results could differ materially due to a number of factors, including those listed on Slide 3 and those outlined in our latest forms 10-K and 10-Q filed with the SEC.
The information we provide about our products and pipeline is for the benefit of the investment community only. It is not intended to be promotional and it is not sufficient for prescribing decisions.
We closed 2017 with another strong quarter, delivering 7% revenue growth, 20% operating income growth and important pipeline progress. Worldwide revenue growth was once again driven by our new pharmaceutical products.
In addition, we continue to expand our margins. Excluding the effects of FX on international inventory sold, gross margin as a percent of revenue increased by roughly 130 basis points, and total expense as a percent of revenue declined by over 340 basis points to 52.8%.
We made progress advancing our pipeline. The FDA-approved and relaunched Taltz for active psoriatic arthritis in the U.S. The European commission approved Taltz for active psoriatic arthritis in the EU. And the FDA accepted our submissions for galcanezumab for migraine prevention as well as the resubmission of baricitinib for rheumatoid arthritis.
On the clinical front, we initiated a Phase III clinical program for baricitinib in atopic dermatitis. We announced that Cyramza did not show an overall survival benefit in first-line gastric cancer and we initiated clinical work on the connected diabetes ecosystem.
Including in trials, we evaluate our automated insulin delivery system as well as development and clinical work on our connected insulin 10 technology.
In terms of capital deployment, we announced an 8% increase in the dividend, reflecting our confidence in the continued growth prospects of the company, and we repurchased $100 million of stock. We closed 2017 with strong momentum and we are well positioned to achieve our strategic deliverables in 2018 and beyond. Slide 5 contains more details on these events as well as other key events since our October earnings call.
I would also note that our analysis of strategic alternatives for Elanco is proceeding well. We're on track to communicate our decision in our Q2 earnings call in July.
This quarter, we've included few additional backup slides on Elanco, where you'll see that recent product launches delivered $40 million of revenue in Q4 and $144 million for the year. We are proud that in January, a leading industry publication announced that Galliprant, a first-in-class anti-inflammatory treatment for canine osteoarthritis pain, was named 2017's best companion animal product, and Clynav, a DNA vaccine for Atlantic salmon took the top honors as the best food animal product.
The product launch momentum continued as Elanco's R&D organization achieve important milestones in January with Galliprant receiving EU marketing authorization, and Credelio, which protects dogs against fleas and ticks, receiving approval in the United States as well as Canada.
We continue to execute on our Elanco business model changes in Q4, including exploring options for the rbST business, exiting select U.S. distribution agreements and taking steps to reduce our manufacturing footprint.
Finally, the biggest news affecting Lilly since our last call is U.S. tax reform. We're pleased that Congress and administration enacted tax reform. It places U.S.-based companies on a more level playing field with our foreign-based competitors. This reform will allow U.S. companies, like Lilly, to be more competitive in the global race for innovation.
For U.S.-headquartered multinational companies, this reform does come with an entry cost through the one-time repatriation toll tax. But it's a net positive as it will enable us access our global cash and will lower our 2018 effective tax rates.
Now I'll turn the call over to Josh to discuss the impact and implications of U.S. tax reform, review our Q4 and full year results and provide an update on our financial guidance for 2018.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext:
Thanks, Dave. On Slide 6, we outlined the financial impact for Lilly. And as stated in our press release, we recognized an estimated charge of $1.9 billion in the fourth quarter related to U.S. tax reform. This charge is comprised of the toll tax assessed on overseas cash and earnings which totaled approximately $3.6 billion, partially offset by the changes in deferred tax which resulted -- resulting from the transition to a U.S. territorial tax system, including the remeasurement of deferred taxes from 35% to 21%.
The other financial impact for Lilly is the effect of our ongoing tax rate. Based on our initial assessment, we expect U.S. tax reform to lower our 2018 effective tax rate by roughly 350 basis points from our prior guidance of approximately 21.5% to about 18%.
The effective tax rate for 2018 reflects to the benefits of the lower U.S. corporate income tax rate, partially offset by other provisions of the new tax form. Our revised 2018 tax rate guidance is subject to change as we further interpret the new law and as subsequent regulations and guidance are issued. 
In total, across both our U.S. and international operations, we estimate that we've now utilized more than $9 billion of cash and investments that won't be required for day-to-day operation.
Essentially, all of this amount is held in US dollars and we do not anticipate any issues in obtaining rapid access to these funds. We do not intend to hold this $9 billion in cash and investments for the long term. Over the course of 2018 and end of 2019, we'll deploy this cash thoughtfully across our capital allocation priorities. First, we'll fund our existing marketed products and pipeline including capital investments in line with our current strategy. Next we'll invest in business development to bolster our future growth prospects, and then we'll return cash to shareholders via increases to the dividend and share buybacks.
While tax reform does provide ready access to additional funds, it does not alter our business development priorities. We'll continue to look for opportunities to augment our pipeline and to bolster our commercial presence in core therapeutic areas of diabetes, oncology, immunology, neurodegeneration and pain. This could come via in-licensing or acquisition. As we stated previously, much of our efforts will be focused on clinical stage assets, creating proof of concepts.
Since the new tax legislation in the U.S. reduces our reliance on debt to fund U.S. cash needs, we will adjust our cash and debt levels going forward. In the near-term, we'll use roughly $2 billion of our repatriated cash to reduce our gross debt level. Finally, we expect to conduct some level of share repurchases under our existing authorization which still has $2 billion remaining.
Hopefully, this gives you a better understanding of the impact of tax reform and how we intend to use our global cash. 
Now let's move to our financial results. Slide 7 summarizes our presentation of GAAP results and non-GAAP measures, while Slide 8 provides a summary of our GAAP results. I'll focus my comments on our non-GAAP adjusted measures, provide insights into the underlying trends in our business so please refer to today's press release for a detailed description of the year-on-year changes in our fourth quarter GAAP results.
Looking at the non-GAAP measures on Slide 9, you'll see the revenue increase of 7% that Dave mentioned earlier. Gross margin as percent of revenue decreased to 76.5%. This decrease was primarily driven by the effect of foreign exchange rates on international inventory sold and product mix, partially offset by manufacturing efficiencies and higher realized prices. Excluding the effect of FX on international inventory sold, gross margin as a percent of revenue increased roughly 130 basis points.
Total operating expense remained essentially flat with marketing, selling and administrative expense decreasing 1% and R&D expense increasing 2%. As a percent of revenue, total OpEx declined by over 340 basis points compared to Q4 2016.
Other income and expense was income of $55 million this quarter compared to income of $16 million in last year's quarter due primarily to higher net gain on sales of investments.
Our tax rate was 20.2%, an increase of 230 basis points compared with the same quarter last year, driven primarily by a lower net discrete tax benefit this quarter compared to Q4 2016.
At the bottom line, net income increased 19% and earnings per share increased 20%. We achieved the significant earnings growth by delivering high single-digit revenue growth while significantly reducing our OpEx ratio, creating positive leverage again this quarter.
Slide 10 details the same non-GAAP measures for the full year, while Slide 11 provides a reconciliation between reported and non-GAAP EPS. You'll find additional details on these adjustments on Slides 25 and 26.
Moving to Slide 12, let's take a look at the effect of price rate and volume on revenue growth. The effect of foreign exchange was minimal this quarter. Excluding a slight tailwind from FX, our worldwide revenue growth on a performance basis was 6% and was primarily driven by volume growth of 4%. It's worth noting that in our human pharma business, each major geography drove volume growth again this quarter.
By geography, you'll notice that U.S. pharma revenue increased 9%, driven by both price and volume. Trulicity, Basaglar and Taltz were the main drivers of this growth, offset partially by the recent losses of exclusivity for Strattera, Effient and Axiron and the decline in volume for Cialis. U.S. price growth in the fourth quarter was favorably impacted by an adjustment for rebates and discounts, primarily related to lower Medicaid utilization across the portfolio.
For U.S. pharma, it's also worth noting that when normalizing for the recent LOEs of Strattera, Effient and Axiron, revenue grew by approximately 20% driven by our new products.
Moving to Europe. Pharma revenue grew 9% excluding FX driven primarily by volume, despite the loss of exclusivity for Cialis and headwinds on Alimta due to competitive pressures, pricing and generic erosion in certain countries. Excluding Cialis and Alimta, the rest of our European pharma revenue grew 22% on a performance basis driven by our new product launch portfolio: Trulicity, Olumiant, Taltz, Jardiance and Lartruvo. 
In Japan, despite the entry of generic Zyprexa last June, pharma revenue increased 9%, excluding FX led by Cymbalta, Trulicity and Cyramza. 
Our pharma revenue in the rest of the world increased 5% on a performance basis this quarter led by Trulicity, Humalog and Forteo.
Turning to animal health. Excluding the impact of FX, worldwide revenue decreased 7% driven by volume. Animal revenue declined 10% driven primarily by market access headwinds for Posilac and competitive pressures for OptiPlex while companion animal revenue was essentially flat.
On a performance basis, excluding the BI U.S. vaccines acquisition, our animal health revenue decreased 11% with companion animal revenue down 16%, driven primarily by a reduction in U.S. distributor inventory levels as well as competitive pressures in parasiticides.
Slide 13 outlines the same information for our full year results. 
Now let's take a look at the drivers of our worldwide volume growth on Slide 14. In total, our new products comprised of Trulicity, Taltz, Basaglar, Lartruvo, Jardiance, Cyramza, Olumiant, Verzenio and Portrazza were the engine of our worldwide volume growth. You can see that these products drove 12.1 percentage points of volume growth. The loss of exclusivity of Cymbalta, Strattera, Effient, Axiron, Zyprexa and Evista provided a drag of 540 basis points, while Cialis and animal health accounted for 170 and 120 basis points of volume decline, respectively.
Slide 15 provides a view of our new product uptake. In total, these brands generated over $1.4 billion in revenue this quarter and represented nearly 23% of our total worldwide revenue, up from 12% in Q4 2016.
Moving on to Slide 16. As mentioned earlier, changes in foreign exchange rates had a minimal effect on our Q4 2017 revenue growth. Similarly, FX had no meaningful impact on our operating expense growth. FX did, however, had a large effect on cost of sales growth and consequently, on operating income and EPS growth. For example, growth in non-GAAP EPS was 20%, including the effect of FX. While it was 32% in constant currency terms, this was largely consistent with the 29% growth for the full year 2017.
Turning to our 2018 financial guidance on Slide 17. You'll see that we've updated our guidance to reflect the estimated impact of U.S. tax reform. This affects our estimated GAAP and non-GAAP tax rates and earnings per share while all other line items remain unchanged. Our revised GAAP to non-GAAP 2018 tax rates are both approximately 18%, while our revised GAAP EPS range is $4.39 to $4.49 and our revised non-GAAP EPS range is $4.81 to $4.91.
I would note the estimated impact of U.S. tax reform on our tax rate and earnings per share guidance is subject to change as we further interpret the new tax law and as subsequent regulations and guidance are issued.
Also in recent weeks we have seen the dollar weaken substantially. If sustained, this weakness would increase the dollar value of our foreign revenue expenses, but due to the effect of FX on international inventories sold, we would likely have only a modest impact on EPS. I will monitor FX movements over the course of Q1 and if appropriate, update our line item guidance in our April call.
Now I'll turn the call back over to Dave to review the pipeline and key future events.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext:
Thanks, Josh. Slide 18 shows select NMEs as of January 24. Movements since our last earnings call include: the initiation of Phase II testing for our D1 potentiator for dementia and our and M3 PEG monoclonal antibody for Alzheimer's disease. Both as monotherapy as well as in combination with an oral BACE inhibitor. Phase I starts for an IDL-1 inhibitor for cancer and IL-33 for immunology and for the automated insulin system I mentioned earlier. As well as the termination of 1 Phase I oncology molecule. 
Our select NILEX pipeline, as shown on Slide 19, reflects the initiation of the Phase III program for baricitinib in atopic dermatitis, and also attrition for Ramucirumab for first line gastric cancer and abemaciclib for squamous nonsmall cell lung cancer.
Turning to Slide 20, you can see the considerable progress we made on the key events we have projected for 2017. While Slide 21 highlights our expected key events for 2018, which we covered on our 2018 guidance call in December.
In addition, noting the approval of Taltz for psoriatic arthritis, you'll see that we've added an event for the expected date of disclosure of the KEYNOTE-189 study, based on Merck's recent press release announcing the positive results of the Phase III study for Alimta in combination with Keytruda. 2018 will be an important year with potential Phase III initiations and data read-outs for several promising new molecules and line extensions. As well as we expect regulatory actions for galcanezumab, baricitinib and abemaciclib.
Before we go to the Q&A session, let me briefly sum up the progress we have made in 2017 and our priorities moving forward. In 2017, new products delivered nearly 20% of our total revenue, up from just 9% 2016. Our growing portfolio of new medicines drove strong 8% top line growth for the full year. We achieved these results while maintaining relatively flat operating expenses, driving operating margin improvement of more than 450 basis points, excluding FX.
We are in the early stages of a growth period driven by revenue from our recently launched products. The potential of our pipeline remains strong with new medicines in development for immunology, oncology, diabetes and neurodegeneration, complemented by a deep, late-stage pain portfolio as well as additional indications for many recently launched products.
Moving into 2018, we remain focused on launching with excellence and replenishing our pipeline while continuing to deliver bottom line growth in operating margin improvement. 
This concludes our prepared remarks. Now I'll turn the call over to Phil Johnson to moderate the Q&A session.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, thank you, Dave. [Operator Instructions] Kingson, if you could provide the instruction for Q&A session, and we are ready to get started.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: [Operator Instructions] We will go to Steve Scala with Cowen.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Two questions. Do you anticipate an FDA adcom for baricitinib by, say, midyear? And can you give us reassurance that there is not any safety issue beyond DVT that will be a subject of discussion? So that's the first question. Second one, is, over the last, say, 2 to 3 decades, Lilly always had 1 to 2 big potential products in the pipeline that investors could focus on. This would appear less a dimension of Lilly today. You probably disagree, so please tell us what billion dollar-plus opportunities you have in the mid-stage pipeline, so not including lanabecestat, galcanezumab, tanezumab or lasmiditan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, Steve, thank you for the question. So Christi if you'll take the question on whether or not we'd expect an FDA adcom for baricitinib and if there's additional safety issues beyond DVT that we think might be addressed in such an adcom be held. And maybe then David and Jan, you'd like to comment on mid-stage pipeline assets that you are excited about. Christi?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Share, hi Steve. As we said before, we do expect the FDA to have an advisory committee, but the timing of that is up to the FDA and they'll make that publicly known. We won't be commenting on a day before they do. And in terms of other safety, we really don't anticipate other major safety questions and issues in regard to the adcom and resubmission.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, thank you, Christi. Dave, Jan?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I can start. Let us start to look at the 2 new Phase II programs that we see. Clearly, we are dependent on more clinical data but we already have some clinical data from Phase Ib in bolstered Z1 potentiator for dementia which is an interesting molecule that could enhance cognition, but potentially also somnolence and in Parkinson's disease, also motor function. The M3 PEG program, is as you probably remember, a plaque-removing antibody. And we have optimized the dosing regimen since it has some immunogenicity and we are putting that now into larger Phase II trials. And we are combining it also with an oral BACE inhibitor which we know that will shutoff the production of amyloid beta. In fact, now, in Alzheimer's, we are trying to get positive data, if at all, doable in Phase II in hundreds of patients, not in thousands of patients in Phase III, and we are addressing early disease. We really want to have a more homogeneous patient population, which has been one of the problems. And we are using our imaging tools all for amyloid and Taltz to make that happen. And we're also trying to hit the target in a maximal way so by not only removing amyloid but stopping the production. The other agent I want to emphasize is potentially with more powerful body weight-lowering effects than the current GLP-1, and that is GIP, GLP-1 coagulates. And we have seen some interesting data in Phase I in healthy volunteers and now we want to see them in a larger Phase II study, how much can these actually reduce body weight and at the same time have a powerful glucose-lowering effect. I also believe that our IL-23 molecule, mirikizumab, has a variety of options for immunological indications, including then IBD, ulcerative colitis where we have potential to be first-in-class. It is also an interesting option for psoriasis and potentially other indications.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Anything to add?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I don't. It's exactly what I would said.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Andrew Baum with Citi.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: A couple of questions. First, for, I guess, Enrique and Lou, since your [indiscernible] we've been expecting a move oncology given Levi is joining the company. There has been more deal that nothing expansive. Were we wrong to assume that there was a capital in balance sheet to accelerating your rebuild in oncology particularly in IO? Or if you could just share with us what are the barriers -- the valuation rather, which may have delayed such activity? That's the first question. Second question for Enrique. We have expressed a fair degree of excitement on the commercial potential of the SGLT2 plus including Jardiance. Perhaps you can give us your perception of where the class could go and what are the barriers to overcome and what catalyst we should achieve pending cardiovascular trial data, [indiscernible] in order to get us there?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Andrew, thank you for the questions. Dave, if you'd like to comment on the external business development strategy for oncology franchise. And then Enrique, the other question on the potential going forward for the SGLT2 class. Dave?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Sure, well, I guess, at a top level, Andrew, I would say your assumptions about our ambition to use balance sheet capacity as well as [indiscernible] capacity to grow our oncology business aren't wrong. We very much plan to do that. We are adding talent, as you noted, and we are looking at many, many opportunities there. Of course, we're bounded by pricing and value which is something we're committed to make sure we're not doing deals just to build strategic holes in the pipeline but to make sense for the investors over the long-term. And I think we also like want to think carefully about our strategy in oncology what can complement existing assets and add to what we're doing versus just de novo efforts to enter a space. So given the price points in oncology, we are careful about our work, but I think it would be reasonable to expect a busy year in business development for oncology for Lilly whether this translate into deals, that's a competitive process, we'll work through that but I don't think directionally your assumptions are wrong.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Dave. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Sure. So I continue to be quite bullish on Jardiance and I think the frame should be much more Jardiance than the SGLT2 class. When we look at the issue of SGLT2 class, we do see a number of headwinds, in particular, because of the updated label for INVOKANA. That has been a headwind for the class but we need to look at Jardiance's growth and overall progression when we look at its use as a much better predictor for that to be a catalyst for the overall SGLT2 class. Clearly, we are focused today on types of diabetes but we do have trial for heart failure and chronic kidney disease. When it comes to type 2 diabetes, some of the challenges that we have had, have been some of those headwinds that I just referred to for what's the leader of the class. We do like the fact that there's going to be increased promotion by having some additional competitors enter the class. I think the reaction is going to be helpful to Jardiance. And finally, just being able to make cardio protection a much higher priority for all physicians beyond A1c when thinking about patients with type 2 diabetes. So we are encouraged by the progress that we continue to see but much more progress is needed.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And next, we'll go to the line of Seamus Fernandez with Leerink.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So just a couple of quick ones. First off, can you guys, I guess, I'm a little confused by the capital allocation discussion. So I guess I'll ask this one in two parts. You guys are reducing your debt to the tune of $2 billion and yet it would seem like -- maybe you can just explain the capital allocation decision there and the need to reduce the debt given your net cash position, so it's a little bit confusing in that regard if you're moving forward with other potential deal considerations. So I'm just trying to get a sense of the discipline there. And the second part of that same question is, has the board already reviewed the capital allocation dynamics and decision points? Or is that coming on a go-forward basis in the wake of tax reform. So you can move on debt immediately but other capital allocation decisions and priorities are under review?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, Seamus, thank you for the questions. On capital allocations, Josh?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Seamus. First, as it relates to our overall position, we're in a very slight net cash position right now. But as I mentioned in the comments, we're going to work that down over time. So we expect overtime to be in a net debt position for sure. I think as it relates specifically to the $2 billion, we have about $13.5 billion of debt and to work that down by $2 billion is really to get our target of 2.5x EBITDA for our leverage ratio. The reason we do that, Seamus, to give us capacity to make investments when we see shareholder value opportunities -- at least opportunities ahead of us. So of course, last year, we've raised, without the new tax reform legislation, we raised debt in the U.S. and actually increased it. So now all we do here is try to get back to our 2.5 leverage ratio and again, that's not a long-term target, that's just where we'd like to start. And then to your question about the board, I mean, we have - we've been very clear, I think, for the last few years about our capital allocation priorities. And as I mentioned, we have $9 billion of cash we now have free access to that we didn't in the past. And we'll work through that cash on a pretty methodical basis and you should expect to see again investments in the business, you should expect to see business development as Dave mentioned, and you will see dividend increases and share buybacks over time.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Josh.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And we'll go to the line of John Boris with SunTrust.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: First question on diabetes. Sequentially, it seems as though there was a higher degree of discounting, rebating going on, so some noise there. Can you maybe just give us some insights into that? And then question for you Dave. I think Lilly has a self-insurance model with some pretty high drug utilization. How do you anticipate, at least, from your expense of having a self-insurance model, how do you anticipate the administration is going to go about lowering drug pricing? Certainly, it seems as though there's a lot of value trap within the system in discounts and rebates, but how would you go about unleashing some of that and getting it back into the hands of employer groups? And more importantly, into patients?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. John, thank you for the question. So Enrique, if you'll comment on insights into what's going on with diabetes rebating here in the U.S. And then Dave, obviously, is going to talk about the very pricing dynamics and how that might be addressed going forward. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Yes, so we continue to see pressure on pricing across all of our diabetes products. When we look at our Q4 result, I think there was pressure, from a rebating perspective, on the instrument portfolio, so both Humalog and Basaglar in particular. But we are being extremely disciplined and we like our overall prospects and the access that we basically have with all of our top brands. We believe that Trulicity, for example, is extremely well positioned for '18 and so is Jardiance.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique. Dave?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, John. Well, I think you're right to point out what's the connection between Lilly's actions as a provider of health care benefits to employees as well as our balance sheet positions. We try to make them as similar as we can. Of course, we do self-insure, we own a financial risk for our beneficiaries and our programs. And I think there's 2 things that we do that stand out from the market. One is we have no bias in our health insurance programs for our employees toward medication, whereas in the general marketplace, on average, patients pay 4x out-of-pocket for medications and other health services. So we think that bias should go away. You can actually make a good argument in medication set a bias for them because of the efficiency of prescription drugs versus other parts of the health care system, but we eliminate that. The other thing which is new for us is rebate passthrough. We've executed for our employee population a pass through a rebate program. We are calling for that through pharma and directly from the government because CMS has unique roles in market maker here and particular in the Part D program. So you would like to see rebate passthrough for Part D beneficiaries. As you know, the rebate levels probably across all pharma are something like 30% to 40%. That could have an immediate and very positive impact on seniors who are struggling to cover their doughnut-hole exposed prescriptions and we think this is a good idea that the administration should act on immediately. I would expect a busy year regulatory wise. In Washington, as they look to introduce market mechanisms and lower cost of the pharmacy counter for patients, well, for both of those things, we will particles close to the administration to try to make a positive progress or innovation way that actually affects.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Dave.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And we'll go to the line of Tim Anderson with Bernstein.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: A couple of questions. In occasional barricades with Trulicity has been your REWIND cardiovascular outcomes trial and then it's high risk because it studies the healthier population so the bar is showing a benefit could, in principle, be higher. Can we play out the scenario whereby this trial does indeed see additional benefit in 2018? In your view, would that have a materially negative impact on the commercial feature of Trulicity, either U.S. or Europe? And if not, why not? And then second question is on oral GLP-1. Your program and your initiatives are much earlier than novos, with nearing the conclusion of both Phase III trials. I'm wondering why there is such a disparity in timelines? Does Novo have a unique technology that Lilly's has a difficulty replicating? Or what's the reason exactly?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, Tim, thank you for the questions. For you, Enrique, on potential impacts, REWIND be negative. And then our efforts on oral GLP-1 and the timing of those.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So clearly, there are a number of cardiovascular trials that are reading out this year. REWIND, but also we have trials for linagliptin in CAROLINA and CARMELINA that are reading out. Lilly has quite a bit of experience when it comes to designing cardiovascular trials in the diabetes space. So we feel good about how we designed the trial. And we are -- continue to be optimistic about our result. I really don't want to speculate about what a negative trial would be. That's clearly not where our case is. Clearly, as we think about future diabetes therapies, having a cardiovascular outcome benefit is going to be increasingly important.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And on the oral GLP-1?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And so we are highly interested in this space and working on pursuing oral GLP-1. And as we think about progressing to the clinical phase, I think the hurdle that we basically have is one of getting the right bioavailability for our product in order to make sure that we can make this commercially successful on a worldwide basis.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Anything you want to add?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Yes, if we look at high level, the oral absorption of peptides is hindered by a lot of natural mechanism. And we assume that the Novo product has only 1% or 2% bioavailability which means you lost most of these substance and the dilemma also remains here about the need for having frosting and not eating for some time after you take this drug because then you have food interaction. So it's really a suboptimal oral agent. We think it would be so much better to have a more traditional small molecule for this receptor.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Jan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Jamie Rubin with Goldman Sachs.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Just stand along the lines of Trulicity and potential changes to that market. Obviously, Ozempic will be launched very shortly if it hasn't already. What sort of changes do you expect to Trulicity's market share as a result of Ozempic? Plus the fact that the [indiscernible] now has a CVD claim as well? And then you touched upon your oral GLP-1 but, obviously, we know we're going to see a lot of oral semaglutide trials readout in 2018. And while there are concerns about the food effects and nausea, if the oral data are successful and essentially replicate Phase II, what impact, if any, do you see oral sema having on your overall diabetes franchise including GLP-1s?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks for the questions, Jamie. So back to you, Enrique, on Trulicity, impact you might expect for Ozempic and the [indiscernible] CV label update. And then thoughts on the potential impact to our franchises if oral semaglutide is successful in Phase III.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So we seem to be very pleased with the performance of Trulicity. Trulicity had significant sequential quarter-on-quarter growth. Our market share for the last week is now over 40% for the first time, so we've seen continued gains in overall share despite the fact that the U.S. also has an indication for a benefit when it comes to CV events. One of the big premises that we think about is really how underutilized the GLP-1 class is today. If we look at, in the U.S. for example, GLP-1s are about 30% of the basal insulin utilization. So we think there's huge room for expansion and we believe that Trulicity is going to benefit from that. We are extremely well prepared for semaglutides launch. I feel good about the experience that we're providing patients. We believe that experience will be unmatched so we do think we will be able to compete very effectively. We don't provide any type of share forecast for any of our products.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And in terms of the oral semaglutide, how might that affect the dynamics within the various franchises that we have?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So first, I think it's extremely important, I think you made some reference to this, when we look at the Phase III trials and what are going to be some of the trade-offs between efficacy, the side effects profile and a view that they are also having realized if this product is going to perform given that may require some strict adherence when it comes to fasting and water intake and so forth. So we need to see more data. Novo has exclusively expressed this desire to price this product comparable to injectables GLP-1, but it's going to position the products very early in the treatment, continue to compete with some of the other orals. So we need to see how that is going to work and given that today, SGLT2s and we look at the value proposition of Jardiance, for example, which is a bit difficult for that product to match. So at the end of the day, how would payers view much different price points for oral GLP-1. So there's a lot of speculation. We need to see more data before we can provide a more educated offense and how successful the product would be in the impact on the risk of diabetes market.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thanks, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Chris Schott with JPMorgan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Just two questions on Taltz, if I could. One, can you just elaborate a little bit more on the opportunity for Taltz in psoriatic arthritis? I think one of your competitors has highlighted this as equal magnitude of the opportunity to the original psoriasis kind of label. I am just wondering how you're thinking about that market as we look at the launch in '18? And the second question on Taltz is about the pricing dynamics in '18. I would have a couple of data points about this being a more competitive pricing environment than we've seen in the past. And just interested on your thoughts on how pricing is going to shake out for this market as we think about this year and longer-term?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Chris. So Christi, can you talk about the opportunity we have seen in psoriatic arthritis and sort of the pricing dynamics as we head into this year?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Absolutely. So we're very excited about the launch of Taltz in psoriatic arthritis. It's already approved in Japan, approved in the U.S. in December, and January in Europe. And the marketer for psoriatic arthritis opportunity would be to look at COSENTYX. More than -- only less than 40% of the scripts for COSENTYX is actually in the dermatology office so we believe this really large opportunity in addition to the ankylosing spondylitis. And we expect that our uptake will be very similar to COSENTYX in TSA as we were in psoriasis. So that market, we think, will be very large. And we think we're competitive. We're the only IL-17 that has, in our label, structure data as well as patients who have not responded well to TMS. And so being very competitive of rheumatologist like the data and they also like the PASI 100 scores. We do think that would be a good market for us. And remember, we're not going after the same customer. We're going after a new entirely office in rheumatology. And then in terms of pricing, in terms of pricing with Taltz this year versus last year and how that relates to access, our access is it will be very similar to last year from a competitive dynamics. This is the marketplace where, unlike others, we actually have a lot of patients that continually turnover. And so as you see with the update of Taltz at launch and continuing, we don't believe that, that will be an issue for us to get an access to patients.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Christi.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of David Risinger with Morgan Stanley.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I have 2 questions. The first is I'm hoping that you can frame the animal health revenue growth prospects in coming quarters. Obviously, the business was down in the fourth quarter. You expect it to flatten out and then return to growth. But if you could help us with our modeling in the next quarter or 2, that would be helpful. And also as part of the commentary, if you could just remind us what the top 2 to 3 new product revenue drivers are in animal health. The second question is, could you comment on the timing of Phase II Alzheimer's read out with cognitive efficacy data. And then third, do you expect the tax rate to decline beyond 2018 as you optimize your tax structuring?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, Dave, thank you for the question. So Jeff, we'll go to you for animal health growth prospects in the coming quarters and some of the key new product drivers. Josh, we'll actually go to you then, give a line for tax rate beyond 2018. An then Jan, we'd like to come in for the timing for fees to read-outs and Alzheimer's space. Jeff?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, David, and kind of at a higher-level on animal health, I would say Q4 results were consistent with our expectations we set and guidance in December. We specifically said then that we expect 2018 revenues to be flat to slightly increasing rest versus 2017. Another note that I would make is fourth quarter revenue decreased 7% excluding FX, but -- and this was driven by volume but importantly, we are starting to see a return to price the growth in the market. We increased at 1% the fourth quarter. So when you look to growth and up to modeling, I would say this, again, 2017, clean food and competition. We underestimated the impact of that. That's was impacted that had driven our results. And some competitive pressures in the companion animal market. So for 2018 specifically, we forecast revenue growth to be flat to slightly up. We expect 2018 to be the year of kind of a transition as we continue to evolve our product mix against headwinds. We see slightly negative growth in the first half of 2018 primarily from the continuing impact of clean food. However, the second half of the year, we expect our 8 launches, and I'll touch on those here, we'll drive top line growth. So within the portfolio, we expect low to mid-single-digit growth in companion animals, offset by a low to single-digit decline in food animals. As Dave noted, I think we will see Galliprant continue to grow. We're seeing close to 30% quarter-on-quarter growth in the canine pain market, we're up to 12% market share. We see that being a key growth driver. I would combine that with last year's launch of INTERCEPTOR PLUS and the share that we've taken from our guard will be another big driver from our business. And then we continue to see our companion animal bar set aside broader portfolio, Credelio, our first take the launch, that is going on this quarter in all 3 major markets, Japan, Europe and the U.S. So Credelio would be the third. And then on the food animal side, we continue to see our vaccine portfolio that we acquired from lumen and salmonella with vaccines being a key growth driver as well. So hopefully that gives you a little guidance on our food in companion animal business, first half, second half of the year, and again, I would emphasize flat to slightly growing in '18.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thanks, Jeff. Josh?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: On tax rate, first, for 2018, as we mentioned, we are estimating about 18%, which is about a 350 point basis point improvement from the prior outlook. Like every other company that's reported or will, I think we could [indiscernible] caveat that by saying that's based on our interpretation and read today of the law, it's very complex. We are still waiting for future guidance and regulations from the IRS. But the base analysis -- the assessment '18, we think 18% is sustainable and we will certainly look to planning to take advantage of the incentives that are built into the law to bring that down over time. So we'll look -- a lot of work still to go there but certainly assume a sustainable 18% in the opportunities to bring that down over time.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Josh. Jan?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: In relation to dementia studies. The symptomatic is in Parkinson's dementia and that has a readout mid 2019. The M3 PEG program, it takes somewhat longer. There is an 18 months treatment there, so in about 2 years.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Jan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Greg Gilbert with Deutsche Bank.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: First, in diabetes, lots of focus on GLP-1, obviously, but Enrique, was curious what you thought the impact might be, if any, when Merck enters with some combo products or at least a combo of DP4 SGLT2? And kind of what you've learned in the marketplace about the market's willingness to use that kind of combo? And what are you expecting from Merck given that they haven't launched something into the space in a while and how that might affect you? And secondly, Dave, lots of guesswork around what you would buy? What you will buy? What you could buy? When it would happen? But hard for you to answer that. But what could you say about how the patient focus of BD, how that has changed since you took over? There's specific things you can point to like changes from the team, changes in priority, changes in the team focus, et cetera, sort of predicting what you're going to do next.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Greg. So Enrique, if you could comment on the potential impact of another DP4 SGLT2 combo coming in. And then Dave, any changes in our approach to BD since you have taken over as CEO. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Very good. So as I mentioned, we view additional competitors coming into the SGLT2 class, paradoxically, as a positive because we believe we are going to be an important catalyst for growth and it's going to help the class but also given Jardiance's strong position, we would likely be the main beneficiary. So your question was specific to the combo, the combo of [indiscernible] with the DP4. We are -- I can't comment on Merck's strategy, but when we look at current practices from physicians prescribing diabetes products, they prefer not to prescribe those combinations. We do have that experience with Glyxambi, so we view that as a long-term proposition for Merck. Now don't know exactly what their strategy is but clearly, for that product, first of all to be successful, they're going to have to establish the benefit of ERK2 diflozin. So we are, of course, prepare the -- we have a number of competitors entering the diabetes space but I feel very good in terms of we are each one of our branch extensively in terms of the benefit that it provides.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique. Greg?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We can't comment on things that haven't happened yet. We do have ambition to step up our game in BD. And we've undertaken a series of things to this year why have we done a bit less through time, where has that occurred in the pipeline space. I think I've been pretty clear through the last year about our ambition in the Phase I, Phase II, so pretty proof of concept, earlier clinical assets is the main target and that's not just because we are excited about that, it's because that's where Lilly is really underrepresented and there's a lot more to go after in price point, which we think are attractive. We have done some specific things in the company. One, public thing which people know about is the realignments of the business department function. Next within our Lilly Research Labs, our R&D organization. I think that's an important change if not psychologically, as we shift our attitude toward this becoming a core part of innovation in our company along with strengthening our own labs. We have also increased resources both human and allocating balance sheet and income statement capacity or yet to be done deals, so that's not a friction as we look at bringing things in. And the final thing I would say is we have core metrics now we're looking at in terms of number of prospects we are evaluating so that we have enough substrate to then execute the right deals across. What hasn't changed is the discipline. We're going undertake to make sure that these are not just strategic without the financial support, but the check all the boxes. The enhance our therapeutic strategies, they can produce, have a good shot at producing strong value for shareholders through time and they fit what we know we do so we can execute once we do bring them in.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Dave.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Umer Raffat with Evercore.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: First, if I may, can you please give us some color into any DDT depth seen with baricitinib perhaps on visibility to how many and the consequence and what happened? And secondly, I know you mentioned in passing, but would love to get your thoughts on real-world usage of the carefully calibrated pre and post-dose staffing [indiscernible] in oral sema and the specific water volumes like how does that play out in a real-world setting based on all the diabetes experience you guys have? If you could comment on that. And then finally, on NGF, my question is from a commercial setting, how do you seek to manage concomitant NSAID usage to mitigate the type 2?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you very much for the questions, let me summarize. So I'm going to start with Enrique actually with that second question on how we see some of the oral sema dosing potential issues playing out in the real world. And then Christi, the other 2 would be for you. Any information we can share on things we've related to DVT deaths with baricitinib in our clinical program. And then how we intend to manage concomitant NSAID use as we would go to market in the future with tanezumab. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: That is a question probably better for Novo than for us. But as a frame, anything that introduces complexity in the lives of people with diabetes as a number of headwinds, so we look to basically develop products so that can appear simply that offer a number of benefits. And that is the mantra of how we think about development of products.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Enrique. Christi?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I would note that there were 2 reported deaths for patients with events of pulmonary embolism in the clinical trial program. One death occurred in the baricitinib group after 523 days after the first dose. And 1 death occurred in the methotrexate group after 224 days methotrexate treatment. Of note, as we have resubmitted and disclosed more data on our recent submission to FDA, one of the things that we look at is the largest pool we have of patients exposed to baricitinib. So all of our clinical trials and also the extension study, we found that in the 2-milligram, the incidence of DVT and PE were 0.5 and in the 4 milligrams it was 0.5 as well. And remember, that's a background rate in RA of 0.3 and 0.8, so we are within the background rate of RA. So that is part of our resubmission package as well as Phase II clinical trials in atopic derma we don't see any as well.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: And then in terms of utilization of concomitant NSAIDs with tanezumab and how that's being managed in clinical trials in how we might envision that playing out upon commercialization.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So we did exclude concomitant use of NSAID in our trials, chronic use. And we'll see what the label looks like once the data comes out and once the FDA approves it and what the label looks like. And then we'll be able to, in the future, be able to tell you how we'll commercialize it.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Christi.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Marc Goodman with UBS.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Just a couple of product questions. Tradjenta looked very strange in the fourth quarter here in the U.S. I was wondering if you could just give us a flavor of what happened there? And then second, on Trulicity, obviously, ramping up nicely in the U.S. I'm just curious if there was anything strange with respect to pricing or inventory in the quarter? Was that just that completely clean quarter relative to the previous 4 quarters, just so we can understand the ramp? And then you mentioned a little bit on Basaglar before but maybe you can also just talk to the past couple of quarters so we can really get a better understanding what the underlying pricing is of this product, including all the rebates.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Mark, thank you for the questions. Enrique, all for you. If you'd like to comment on some of the U.S. dynamics we're seeing for Tradjenta, Trulicity and Basaglar.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Very good. So let me start with Tradjenta. We did have a favorable impact due to our changes in estimates for rebates and discounts for the quarter, in the case of Tradjenta, roughly $10 million as it relates to Lilly revenue. You may recall that in Q3 of 2017, so last quarter, we actually had a favorable adjustment. So sequentially, it does look like an anomaly. You asked about Basaglar. We do see very strong sequential growth for Basaglar, 24%, TRx quarter-on-quarter. We do have high rebates. Part of this is basically a channel accrual when it comes to -- given the expected increased utilization in part D starting January 1. And by the way, we've seen excellent uptake. When we look at Basaglar today, at the first few weeks, we're now basically capturing about 25% of the new patients in the basal insulin class. Excited about the growth prospects. And then when we look at Trulicity, there were some volume, roughly about $25 million when it comes to inventories. Outside of that, we do see a lot of movement when it comes to gross net and so forth. What I will say when we look at our diabetes products is to look at them really more over time and trying to get a complete picture by looking at rolling quarters. But we are, once again, excited about the sequential growth Trulicity is having.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Geoff Meacham with Barclays.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Dave, I know you're still on the Elanco strategic review, but how does the tax policy or the new product launches change your view of its internal value? Or is it still about its margin contribution? And then a couple of product questions. One, for Forteo, good for few trends for a late cycle product. Just help us with an insight for 4Q and then going forward. And then on abemaciclib, how does a recent pricing action we saw with brands in Europe impact either your opportunity or your investment there?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, Jeff, thank you for the question. So Dave, we'll start with you on question of some of the impact of tax policy as well as factors on our Elanco decision pricing. Sue, if I can then go to you to talk about some of the recent announcements that were made on pricing or competitor product in the space in Europe. And then Christi on the Forteo question here in the U.S. Dave?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks for your question. On Elanco, more or less, since we announced the strategic review in October, the basic assumptions and the way we're conducting it haven't really changed. Of course, tax makes everything a little bit more valuable in that can temporize as our thinking. Elanco, I think, there's a 2-part story. One is innovation on the top line and I think we've commented on that this quarter. I think there's been great progress. And both introduction of new products as well as approvals recently. That's a piece of does that we are factoring going forward is a growing company in animal health. And in the margin expansion is a significant opportunity for animal health and one of the team is very focused on. So all the things are true. I think, of course, we must ask what's the available path forward for the shareholders to hold, to spend, to partner in some other way and that's still ongoing. But more or less, the direction of those assumptions is at the same when we started this, and that's good news as we continue to work through that project.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thanks, Dave. Sue?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I can't comment on announcement. What I can say is we are ready for our launch in Europe. We submitted last year in Europe and in Japan. We are hopefully expecting approval in both those geographies either this year. We are prepared for launching with the Fed for appropriate access and reimbursement for patients where there is a considerable unmet need in Europe. Just an update on the U.S., the update so far has been very promising. We feel really good about the performance to date and that is based on the MONARCH 1 and MONARCH 2 population. That's about 30% of the patients available and we are anticipating approval in the first half of this year based on the MONARCH 3, that separately indication. Again, the performance is good so far. And we feel very confident in the U.S. performance as well as the opportunity in Europe and Japan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Sue. Christi?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So Our U.S. sales in Forteo in Q4 grew 32% versus the Q3 of 13%. So thank you for the question because there was some unique dynamics in Q4. One was we benefited from the net price adjustments, rebates and discounts related to Medicaid. whereas in most quarters, rebates and discounts lower the realized benefit from those price increases. And the second thing was that in terms of the volume exhilaration, we had a wholesaler buy-ins that led to an increase in the volume for Q4.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Christi.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: The next question comes from the line of Jason Gerberry with Bank of America.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Just 2 for me. Firstly, on Alimta. Your thoughts or expectations if you were to lose the patent suit with the alternative form, the 505B2 generic, just curious your thoughts on sort of what type of market share you think a low cost an alternative salt form could capture in the oncology setting? I don't know if you have any analogs but with the trial starting tomorrow, I'm just curious of your thoughts there. And then my second question on the diabetes front. Obviously, a conversion of [indiscernible] to higher cost brands have been kind of a value driver in this space more broadly. And just trying to get a little bit of a sense for the CAROLINA study. If that's, say, positive, you maybe see that as something that drives more conversion to DPP4? Or do you see that as a broader catalyst depending upon the cardiovascular profile for use to other proprietary classes, I guess, GLP2 or oral GLP-1.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So sue, if you could provide some thoughts on the potential impact if we were to use the alternate salt form case starting soon. And then Enrique, your thoughts on CAROLINA, if positive, how might that affect both DPP4 as well as SGLT utilization. Sue?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: With regard to the alimta patent, as you said, we got the alternative salt form hearing this week in the District Court of Indiana. And we feel really confident with regards to our case here and we believe that we can continue to defend the patent and feel confident in our opportunities to do that. Clearly, in a case that didn't happen, we would look at a revised guidance. But at this point in time, we will continue to drive Alimta. With the KEYNOTE-189 data being presented later this year, we think we have a great opportunity to really continue to consolidate Alimta as a standard of care in first-line setting and the preferred chemo in combination with IL.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Sue. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So represent about 20% of the overall oral diabetes markets. As a class, as a share of the market, that is declining, certainly declining over time. Clearly a positive CAROLINA trial will significantly accelerate that. My view is most classes basically will benefit. It will not be limited to DPP4s and into in particular but we will also see acceleration of both SGLT2s in particular Jardiance and GLP-1 class.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: We'll go to the line of Vamil Divan with Crédit Suisse.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I have a question about these programs that we're hearing more about. Some plans were the co-pay support manufacturers provide [indiscernible] mutuals deductible. I'm just wondering if you could comment on what you're seeing around this issue? And is there anything you think other products can do to offset the pressure? Because I was just wondering specific drugs about Taltz for that question. And second one on your CGRP, I'm just curious if you could share a little more in terms of commercial presentations at product in terms of the needle gauge, the device some of basically trying to differentiate them product some of your competitors in terms of how we develop.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Great, Vamir. Thank you very much for the question. Dave, if you could comment on the question about [indiscernible] and then Christi, whatever you could share, you might not be able to fully respond, but what can you share about the commercial presentation for CGRP. Dave?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Well, the idea that get credited differently than in the past. Is actually not really the ideal when you're talking about this year. We are in the back-and-forth between manufacturers and material ways to rebalance that. I think the overall concerning thing is the continued shift across our consumers. Recently, by implementing this program without rebate passthrough, which I think would be an important compensating measure, it just increases the exposure that patients have to their high deductible plans and that's a bad idea, in our minds. So policy-wise, we are not for it. There are technical ways to wait and then you can rest assured we are implementing those as far as Taltz and others. There will be, I think, and throwing at this points 3 time. I think the bigger issue for the country is how do we make chronic medications patient made more affordable for them and then we go back to the rebate pass-through. That increasing spread is a big issue for patients that has less price deductible plan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Dave. Christi?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So we have a strong history, obviously, in the autoinjector, and that would be our plan from the commercialization standpoint with galcanezumab.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: I wanted to stick with CGRP if I may and simply ask is this -- would you expect utilization to be principally for those who would be chronic users oral injection once per month? headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018

componenttext: So we expect most of the uses will be preventive, obviously. There's about 4 million to 5 million patients in the U.S. alone that are on preventive measures. But we also estimate that about 15 million patients could be eligible because they have fallen off their preventive medicine for one reason or the other, efficacy or safety. headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks for the questions, Jeff. So our Jeff, could you comment please on Elanco trends that you see going forward and outlook for longer-term growth as well as margin expansion. headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: As you've noted, Jeff, good questions. We have really 3 priorities to our strategy as we go forward and we are definitely keeping in contact as we go forward that assessment is: one, accelerating innovation; two is changing our mix to higher growth and margin segments; and three, this margin expansion plan, those being the 3. I think as Dave noted, we feel very good about our pipeline and launching products that we're in the midst of right now. That will be what will return us to, we believe, higher-quality growth and higher margins going forward. So as I've noted, it will be flat to slightly growing in '18 and we continue to see that increasing going forward. I think the margin expansion story's a significant one. There's really a couple of points to that. One is just cost initiatives and we pulled most of the key levers that we see here in the near and medium term as well as our footprint. So we have announced 2 things this year, continue to look at rightsizing our footprint after the integration. So the large 1 consolidation as well as Augustine, I think the bST assessment would help. We hit $1 billion in comp animals in 2017 for the first time. I think just the example of the contrast of the bST into the comp animals is a demonstration of this mix change. We have said very clearly on margins that we do see, as we said in December 15 of the investor conference, we are turning to 30% operating margins albeit it will be taking a little bit longer given some of these forces like clean food. But we do see a path to the margin expansion, so increasing as we go forward in the short and medium-term.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Jeff. Christi?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So Tremfya has had a similar launch uptake as what we've seen with other biologics in the psoriasis space. And as we've said before, we believe that the newer agents and what we're seeing in the marketplace is the newer agents are really increasing expectations of physicians, of patients to ensure, to release its patients more quickly from older agents to the newer ones. In fact, the last 2 years, the market growth has been 15%, unlike the years before where we're single digits. So we think that's a good thing. And then on the COSENTYX price adjustment, we continue to look very closely at that. What we've seen and what we thought launched in spite of that having great access is because patients actually cycle of routinely, go from 1 each agent to another. We haven't had to utilize that piece but will continue to keep an eye on it and ensure that we continue to get access for patients were probably 100 clean skin scores.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Jan. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: So some of the additional entrants into the BACE space are held up right now due to litigation. I think that is a question really for them and for Sanofi. As it relates to interchangeability, we think this will eventually happen. The right studies will need to be conducted with a view these as years away. There's nothing imminent.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Thank you. Dave?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
componenttext: Yes, rebate passthrough is good. Everyone is talking about it because it should happen. If it weren't already happening in commercial plans. I know that because we are executing it in our plans and I know other large employers are as well. It is not happening in party. We have on the table for comments in the fall. I think a number of centers and others have weighed in on that. There is a push and pull there between should we allow a modest increase in premiums in Part D to support the funding of rebate passthrough. Now we're for that because, of course, not passing through the rebate subjects the very ill to the cost burden versus spreading that over a much larger base. We think that's what insurance is for, and therefore, we [indiscernible] It's just difficult to speculate on the probability that happening. But I could tell you there's a strong unanimity amongst large manufacturers. It is a serious proposal being looked at in both the hill and HSS. And to me, it is one of the simplest levers to pull to actually change the cost of the pharmacy counter for drugs in America. And I think the U.S. should do it. That doesn't mean it will happen though, so we'll have to stay close on that one and we'll keep you updated.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q4 2017 Earnings Call, Jan 31, 2018
