headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Very good. So let's start with the easies one. Tradjenta net sales in the U.S. reflect the trend that we see when it comes to total prescriptions. Basically, it's a steady number of prescriptions quarter-on-quarter. And that, basically what we see when it comes to net sales. When it comes to Jardiance, just maybe to provide a bit more color. We are showing the case of Jardiance a 58% TRS growth versus the previous year quarter-on-quarter. On a sequential basis, that growth is 13%. So very solid growth when it comes to Jardiance. But the net sales, there's a disconnect there because the net sales for the quarter versus the previous year only increased 28%. We did have an adjustment to -- due to changes in estimates for rebates and discounts. Normalizing for that, the gross versus the previous year would have been 46%. Now when we look on a sequential basis, in the case of Jardiance, some of the changes and the estimates were previous discounts really belonged in Q1, so you basically have a double whammy effect when you look at the sequential quarter-on-quarter comparison.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique. Jan?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: In the generation portfolio, respectively, of the neurodegeneration targeted molecules in Phase II trials, starting with D1 PAM. This is designed to be a symptomatic of molecule for patients suffering with dementia. Initially we are testing it in Parkinson disease. We have recently believed that there is a higher probability of success in that population but if we get a positive signal there, we would very quickly move that also in parallel to Alzheimer's disease. So that's the first. The other 2 that you've mentioned, the next one behind it is N3pG, that's an anti-plaque A-beta antibody based on what we saw in some pretty extended Phase 1 trials. This molecule can clear plaques really to a very dramatic depth, so a great degree of clearance. And also with a great amount of speed, so faster clearance I think that we have seen with other mechanisms. So we're excited about that. We're testing that as monotherapy. We're also testing it in conjunction in combination therapy. I believe it's the first combo trial of 2 disease-modifying drugs in Alzheimer's disease with a BACE inhibitor. With the thought there that although BACE monotherapy has been disappointing, if you turn off A-beta production also clear out the plaque that could have added benefits, so that trial is enrolling. And then we also have the Tau monoclonal antibody. This is specific for aggregated forms of Tau and we think this is an important therapeutic modality in Alzheimer's. Both of those Phase II, all 3 of the Phase II trials are designed to give us efficacy signals both on biomarkers and on cognitive endpoints. I think on clinicaltrials.gov, we showed the disease modifying trials ending in 2020 to 18 months treatment period but depending on enrollment rates and depending on a potential per interim analysis, this also have a potential to data readouts earlier, even next year. But we'll have to wait and see how these trials go.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Dan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: And the next question is from the line of, one moment please, it's going to be Jami Rubin, Goldman Sachs.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: David, just want to go back to some of the questions around rebate structures. And I recognize that it's early and it's hard to predict how this is going to play out but just when you look at your own numbers and think about different scenarios, looking at filing for Lilly's gross-to-net of 48% is the widest in the industry largely because of your diabetes franchise. Is If is a change to those rebates, what would you expect to happen to volumes? You just said that volumes are -- that rebates are used to induce volumes, so do you expect volumes to go down for that business? Conversely, if rebates go away, Lilly has about $10 billion in rebates. How can this not be a net positive even if you take into consideration lower volumes and potentially lower list price. How are you thinking about the different scenarios that can play out here?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Great, Jami, thank you for the question. Dave?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Jami. I mean, the huge opening caveat here is we don't know, right? We need -- we don't know what this rule is, we know where the system will go. We paused on the list price change in the U.S. waiting to see what's happening here. But just to get the numbers right, so we reported last year a 51% gross-to-net spread. You're right in that the diabetes segment, in particular, drive that, primarily because you have a very large Medicaid population and you have a very large gross-to-net spread in Medicaid approaching the federal cap of 100%. So and we've been pretty open about all that. It depends on how this will be enacted, whether we change the Medicaid system, whether we would treat that commercial et cetera to really guess on what would be on the other side of it. As I said in my first response though, we make innovative products. Hopefully, products people want and need. I think the current system has resulted in basically a structure of the industry where the hospital systems, insurance carriers, distributors and manufacturers seem to be fine with the current system. The problem is we're shifting too much cost via list pricing directly to consumers. I have to believe that if consumer pricing came down, it would improve volume, improve medication adherence, improve outcomes for patients. So we think, that's why, in general, we think this direction of travel is a good way. Now we have to see all the specifics because clearly there's a lot of room for disruption in a negative way here and it would be very difficult for Lilly to predict what would happen to the gross-to-net line you're talking about or more specifically without seeing the totality of the regulatory reforms.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Is there a scenario there where this is a net positive for the industry even if volumes do go down but your rebates go away as well?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: I think there's a lot of scenarios, Jami. It's hard to speculate.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Jami.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: And next, we have Geoff Meacham, Barclays.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018

Is there a category that you feel you still need to add assets like, for example, in oncology, to be more competitive?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thank you for the question. So Dan, maybe if you can comment on strategies going forward for the 4 mg in RA and how that may compare with other NILEX opportunities that we're evaluating. And then Dave, obviously, the comment on the business development priorities we have moving forward. Dan?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thanks. We're excited about the potential of baricitinib for patients potentials being realized, of course, to a great extent outside the U.S. in case both doses are approved. In the [indiscernible] I think we were clear in our position about the benefits that each dose could have for patients and we were clear [indiscernible] in our disappointment. I think in terms of next steps for the 4-milligram dose, we're still considering what different possibilities might be internally at this time. I think that in terms of the prioritization here, there's great opportunities for baricitinib in RA. But also in some of the other indications. So the lupus data, which was recently published in Lancet, I think represents a really great opportunity for patients and an opportunity here for us to be first-in-class here in lupus. We are also excited about atopic derm, where we now have a Phase III trial ongoing. So lots of opportunity for patients with [indiscernible]

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: I would just add, that we've launched just now, started promoting in July in the U.S. the 2 milligram anecdotal feedback's very positive. And we know from the German launch and French launches which is ongoing, that [indiscernible] rapid pain relief. This is a different modality than are used in disease modifiers for RA and we are far from disappointed with the performance of the molecule at a global basis. More to do in the U.S. we'll update investors with probably more detail on the Q3 call on that performance and the life cycle, as Dan pointed out, is potentially quite broad. On the second question of BD priority, I hate to say this, but I think you got it exactly wrong. I wouldn't attribute it all that we are satisfied with BD. We are in a position now with we are growing the top line, we are growing income, we are growing cash flows. We are very interested in acquiring assets that add to all of our therapeutic areas of interest. We're not plugging a hole here. It's really about building value for the long term. And because we are optimistic about the short and midterm in terms of growth prospects, we're in a position to do just that. I would expect to see a continuous flow of pipeline adds from Lilly, hopefully from our own labs, but if not, from outside labs as well. And we have more than enough resources to execute that strategy.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Dave.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: So Humalog had an excellent quarter. We did have a benefit in Q2 due to changes in the estimates for rebates and discounts. That benefit was relative to the previous year, was about 9 points, so an important benefit. Now as we-- it's difficult for us to be able to project forward but we are seeing a bit of a benefit also when it comes to mix. Now as it relates to [indiscernible] it's very difficult to, for us, to forecast and predict how the competitor would basically play in the market. Clearly, at this point in time, we basically see them as gaining share, in particular in the area of managed Medicaid. But it's difficult for us to predict what the access and uptake they would be able to have in 2019.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Great.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: As it relates to access in the migraine category, it's pretty early days. I understand mostly players have enlisted the new therapy. We are, of course, heard the guidance from the FDA in early conversations with payers about our data and seeking that kind of feedback from them. I can tell you what our strategy is which is really broad access to these medications as appropriate. And particularly given the population, so these are mostly commercially insured working women who are having anywhere between 4 and 20 headaches a month, that's our study population, which causes absenteeism, debilitation, lack of ability to predict and schedule and plan, not to mention just the human suffering cost. So we think employers will be very interested in covering this class. We need to get that message through. It could be a great category for some value and outcomes-based pricing approaches and we are optimistic long-term that the class have good coverage.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Enrique, any from your conversations with payers to add?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thank you for the question. So Sue for you on both Verzenio and Alimta updates.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thanks for the question. We're really pleased with the update on Verzenio today. The sales this quarter was 57.7 million, that's 28% -- a $28 million increase this last quarter. What we're seeing is, as you know, we are the third to market CDK, but we are second now in terms of both NRx, New to Brand and also in TRx. In this quarter, our MBRX share was 18.7% and we continue to see that increase. So we are pleased to update. What we're seeing is when proficiency using the brand, they like it. So our focus is on ensuring that we continue to get a trial of Verzenio. And so far, we have about 1,500 physicians who have chart Verzenio. The patients that are using is across the spectrum in the front line and we do have some activity but really we are starting to see much more now uptake in the front line so with the [indiscernible] inhibitors as well as second line in progression. And the things that are resonating is the continuous dosing. The simulation activities is clearly a differentiator and people also interested. And I think the data is resonating in the fact that we see positive data in patients who got characteristic. So we feel pretty good so far about the uptake in Verzenio. We see opportunity to continue to grow that brand. the market is growing about 22% and we are only about 15% of patient is still getting a CDK 4 6 inhibitor. With regards to Alimta, we've got the data update now for the KEYNOTE-021G is now in the label. We have also submitted to the FDA to include the KEYNOTE-189 data. What we are seeing is, as you heard, we are growing Alimta in the U.S. We had a 2% growth based on volume, 1% price and 3% overall. And although the data we have presented at ASCO fairly recently, since that time, we are seeing an increase in new patients and prescriptions. So as a reminder, about 50% of Alimta use is in the sort of in promoted areas. We do have Alimta some of the use for the KEYNOTE-189 regimen is sort of in addition, Alimta use that we've got now but we're clearly also seeing an increase in the use and we continue to believe that we'll see that going forward.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Sue.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: So Trulicity today is having fantastic growth. Previously, GLP-1 market is growing very fast at 26% for Trulicity so also having continued share growth over the last few months. We have an excellent access position. And quite frankly, an unmatched patient experience when we look at the real world efficacy that Trulicity delivers, and we look at patient adherence. And very simply delivered. So very excited about the poor core of the product. Clearly, REWIND, as we said before, is an important trial for us and we believe that we designed this trial in the appropriate way. We expect that we'll going to have a top line sometime by the end of the year. But we -- one of the differences for REWIND versus other trials is the time the patients are going to be on the product. This is not a short product, it's fairly long, and we expect that patients would be, on average, would be 5 years on Trulicity. So we look forward to the results of the trial. But at this point in time, we are not going to speculate.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique. Dan?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: With tanezumab, of course, with Pfizer, our priority here in the current slate of trials is to demonstrate the benefit risks of this drug for patients in a variety of settings. Having said that, your question was sort of broader about the societal impact of the opioid crisis and the need for nonopioid alternatives for chronic pain. Of course, we also see that. That's one of the reasons we're excited by the potential of the pain portfolio. One of the reasons that some years ago we decided to invest in the future for tanezumab. I think how regulators will see the interplay between new molecules for pain versus the opioid crisis is important and we think that we have an important role to play their and helping solve that problem.

componenttext: So Josh, if you want to respond to the question in terms of what has caused Lilly to actually lever up?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: The data offering, the intent at this point is to do that before we launch the IPO of less than 20%. Obviously, over time, that sits on Elanco's books. I think if you look at Lilly's capital allocation priorities and cash flow, we do expect to be more levered over time as a function basically of tax reform. If you'll recall that prior to tax reform, we were in a position that we had cash that was restricted in how we can use it. And that was, in fact, trapped overseas. As that has freed up, we don't need to keep as much cash, so you should expect to see Lilly on a net basis be more levered. And in fact, in Q2, we moved into a net debt position. So it's less an issue of what will happen with Elanco because that will normalize over time and more a function of our capital structure, which is now gives us much more flexibility as we're able to manage and use cash around the world.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018

We would lose a certain amount of EBITDA, although there would not be a significant reduction to our debt. So there will be a modest, maybe 20 basis point increase in the debt-to-EBITDA kind of numbers that we have. A very minimal change in terms of Lilly's leverage moving forward.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: No further questions in queue at this time.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: Excellent. Thank you very much. Dave, if you'd like to go ahead and conclude the call with some comments.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q2 2018 Earnings Call, Jul 24, 2018
componenttext: We appreciate your participation in today's earnings call and your interest in Eli Lilly and Company. Our strong first half growth makes us increasingly confident in our ability to deliver 5% compound revenue growth from 2015 to 2020, and to achieve a 30% operating margin in 2020. While our first half pipeline accomplishments continue to highlight the depth and breadth of our prospects for growth beyond 2020. As demonstrated most recently by our decision on Elanco, we continue to take concrete actions to focus our resources and allocate capital to best serve our customers and create value for our stakeholders. With a diverse set of new product opportunities to drive growth, a strong R&D capability and a clear path to margin expansion, we believe Lilly continues to be a compelling investment. Thanks, again, for dialing in. Please follow up with our IR team if you have questions we have not addressed on the call. And you all have a great day.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext:
Ladies and gentlemen, thank you for standing by, and welcome to the third quarter 2018 earnings call. [Operator Instructions] As a reminder, this conference is being recorded. 
I'd now like to turn the conference over to our host, Mr. [indiscernible] please go ahead.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext:
Thank you, and good morning. Thank you for joining us for Eli Lilly and Company's Q3 2018 earnings call. I'm Dave Ricks, Lilly's Chairman and CEO. Joining me on today's call are Josh Smiley, our Chief Financial Officer; Dr. Dan Skovronsky, the President of Lilly Research Labs and our Chief Scientific Officer; Enrique Conterno, the President of Lilly Diabetes and Lilly U.S.A.; Christi Shaw, the President of Lilly Bio-Medicines; and Ann White, who is joining us for the first time as our new President of Lilly Oncology. We're also joined by Kristina Wright, Mike, Kevin Hern and Phil Johnson of the IR team.
Please note that Josh Simmons is not on the call today as Elanco is now having their own quarterly earnings calls, including their first call earlier this morning. Subsequent questions regarding their business performance and outlook should be directed to Elanco's investment relations team.
During this call, we anticipate making projections and forward-looking statements based on our current expectations. Our actual results could differ materially due to a number of factors, including those listed on Slide 3 and those outlined in our latest forms 10-K and 10-Q filed at the SEC. The information we provide about our products and our pipeline is for the benefit of the investment community. It is not intended to be promotional, and it is not sufficient for prescribing decisions.
We continued our strong performance in 2018 with a third quarter revenue growth of 7% non-GAAP -- and non-GAAP operating income of 29% and non-GAAP EPS growth of 32%. New pharmaceutical products, which now represent 35% of human pharma revenue in the quarter, continue to be the driver of our worldwide growth, led by Trulicity, Taltz and Verzenio. New products continue to drive strong volume-based growth, highlighted by the 14% pharma volume growth and the 30% U.S. diabetes volume growth.
We continue to expand our margins this quarter. Excluding the effect of FX on international inventories sold, non-GAAP gross margin as a percent of revenue increased by nearly 110 basis points over Q3 2017, and non-GAAP operating income as a percent of revenue increased by over 380 basis points to 28.1%.
We made significant progress for the pipeline this quarter including the approval and launch of Emgality in the U.S., a positive CHMP opinion for Emgality from Europe; the submission of lasmiditan in the U.S. as well as positive Phase III readouts for Ultra Rapid Lispro in type 1 and type 2 diabetes patients and a positive Phase II read out for GIP/GLP-1, which is now called in type 2 diabetes.
And we announced yesterday that Trulicity demonstrated superiority in the reduction of major adverse cardiovascular events across a broad range of people with type 2 diabetes in the precedent-setting REWIND trial. These results are important for the millions of people with type 2 diabetes who face a higher risk for CV disease.
We will submit this data to regulatory authorities and plan to present the detailed data at ADA in 2019. These extended data, along with Trulicity's proven powerful efficacy, simply delivered further validate Trulicity as a well-established option for people with diabetes.
In terms of capital deployment, Elanco Animal Health became a publicly traded company via a well-received initial public offering. Through the IPO and associated get offering, Elanco raised over $4 billion in capital. The vast majority of which was provided to Lilly as partial consideration for the Animal Health business that Lilly transferred to Elanco in connection with the IPO.
We announced several business development transactions, including a licensing agreement with Chugai Pharmaceuticals for an oral GLP-1 agonist, licensing and research collaboration with Dicerna Pharmaceuticals, utilizing their RNAi technology platform. A collaboration with NextCure, Inc., focused on discovery and development of novel immuno-oncology cancer therapies. And we purchased a Priority Review Voucher. We returned nearly $600 million via the dividend and we repurchased 100 -- or $1 billion of stock.
Moving on to Slides 5 and 6, you'll see more detail on key events since our July earnings call.
Now I'll turn the call over to Josh to review our Q3 results and to provide an update on our financial guidance for 2018.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext:
Thanks, Dave, and good morning. Slide 7 summarizes our presentation of GAAP results and non-GAAP measures, while Slide 8 provides a summary of our GAAP results. I'll focus my comments on our non-GAAP adjusted measures to provide insights into the underlying trends in our business, so please refer to today's earnings press release for a detailed description of the year-on-year changes on our third quarter GAAP results.
Looking at the non-GAAP measures on Slide 9, you'll see the revenue increase of 7% that Dave mentioned earlier. Gross margin as a percent of revenue increased to 76.7%. Excluding the effect of foreign exchange rates on international inventories sold, gross margin as a percent of revenue increased nearly 110 basis points, primarily driven by manufacturing efficiencies, and to a lesser extent, the favorable impact of product mix, partially offset by the negative impact of price on our revenue.
Total operating expense increased roughly 1%, with marketing, selling and administrative expense, increasing 2%, while R&D expense which remained relatively flat. Total operating expense as a percent of revenue compared to Q3 2017 declined 275 basis points to 48.8%, benefiting from previously announced actions taken to reduce the company's base cost structure.
Operating income increased 29% compared to Q3 2017, which put our operating margin at 27.9% for the quarter. And as Dave mentioned earlier, excluding the effect of FX on international inventories sold, our operating income was 28.1% of revenues, an improvement of over 380 basis points versus last year's quarter.
Other income and expense was an expense of $15.4 million this quarter compared to income of $49.9 million in last year's quarter. Our tax rate was 15.1%, a decrease of 380 basis points compared with the same quarter last year, driven primarily by the impact of U.S. tax reform. At the bottom line, net income increased 21%, while earnings per share increased slightly faster at 32% due to reduction in shares outstanding from share repurchases.
We achieved a significant earnings growth by delivering high single-digit revenue growth, while carefully managing our operating expenses, significantly improving profitability again this quarter.
Slide 10 details these same non-GAAP measures for September year-to-date, while Slide 11 provides a reconciliation between reported and non-GAAP EPS. You'll find additional details on these adjustments on Slides 23 and 24.
Moving to Slide 12. Let's take a look at the effect of price, rate and volume on revenue growth. This quarter, foreign exchange had a modest effect on revenue. Our worldwide revenue growth on a performance basis was 8%, driven by a 12% increase in volume, partially offset by price. Q3 represented the seventh straight quarter our human pharma business delivered volume growth in each major geography.
U.S. pharma revenue increased 11%, driven by impressive volume growth of 17%, led by Trulicity, Basaglar, Taltz and Verzenio, partially offset by price. And Dave mentioned earlier our U.S. diabetes business delivered 30% volume growth yet again this quarter. This performance affirms our volume-based growth strategy is critical as we grow through an evolving U.S. price environment. You will see U.S. price decline 6% this quarter. Nearly 3 points of the U.S. price decline was driven by Nulasta and corresponding volume for Basaglar in Medicare part D, which we didn't have in last year's quarter.
We also have approximately 2 points of decline associated with increases to our patient affordability and access programs for Taltz and Humalog, which also drove increased volumes. The remaining point of decline is the net effect of changes to estimates and rebates and discounts across segments, partially offset by the impact of price increases we took late last year and early this year.
Moving to Europe. Pharma revenue grew 1%, driven by volume, largely offset by price. This volume growth was achieved despite the loss of exclusivity for Cialis. Excluding the impact of the Cialis LOE, volume grew over 15%. This robust volume growth was led by Olumiant, Trulicity, Taltz, Lartruvo and Jardiance.
In Japan, pharma revenue decreased 2% with volume growth more than offset by price and the impact of the biannual price cuts which took effect in Q1. Volume growth was driven by new products namely Trulicity, Olumiant, Taltz and Jardiance, with a significant contribution also coming from Cymbalta.
Our pharma revenue in the rest of the world increased 15% on a performance basis this quarter, led by volume growth for new products namely Trulicity, Cyramza, Jardiance, Lartruvo, Taltz and Basaglar as well as strong volume growth from Humalog.
Turning to animal health. Total worldwide revenue grew 6% on a performance basis this quarter driven by higher prices and higher volume. Higher sales of companion animal disease prevention, ruminants and swine and companion animal therapeutics products were partially offset by lower sales of products that are being exited, including the products which Lilly has retained control of after the Elanco IPO.
Slide 13 outlines the same information for our September year-to-date results.
Now let's take a look at the drivers of our 12% worldwide volume growth on Slide 14. In total, our new products, including Trulicity, Taltz, Basaglar, Verzenio, Olumiant, Jardiance, Lartruvo and Cyramza were the engines of our worldwide volume growth. You can see that these products drove 13.8 percentage points of volume growth this quarter. This robust pharma volume represented our strongest quarterly volume performance this decade. A loss of exclusively for Strattera, Effient, Zyprexa, Axiron, Evista and Cymbalta provided a drag of 110 basis points while Cialis accounted for 190 basis points of volume decline.
At the end of September, generic versions of Cialis entered the U.S. market which is expected to result in rapid erosion of sales. When excluding LOEs and Cialis, the rest of our products had volume growth of approximately 19%.
Siloed 15 provides a view of our new product update. In total, these brands generated nearly $1.9 billion in revenue this quarter, representing 31% of our total worldwide revenue and 35% of our human pharmaceutical revenue.
With the launch of Emgality and the submission of lasmiditan, we look forward to adding a fourth leg to our new product growth story, joining recent launches in diabetes, oncology and immunology and demonstrating that Lilly is also building an innovative pain franchise.
Moving to Slide 16. Continuing with our non-GAAP explanations, this quarter, the effect of FX had a relatively minimal impact on our income statement, with a small negative impact on revenue, and a small positive impact on earnings.
Turning to our 2018 financial guidance on Slide 17, you will see that we updated our non-GAAP guidance to reflect the continued strong operational performance. We're narrowing the range for revenue to $24.3 billion to $24.5 billion, narrowing the range for SG&A expense to $6.3 billion to $6.5 billion, decreasing our tax rate from 17% to 16%, due primarily to recently issued guidance on elements of U.S. tax reform; and we're raising and lowering our non-GAAP earnings per share range to $5.55 to $5.60.
At the midpoint of the ranges represent an increase of 30% over 2017. I would note that this revised EPS range assumes a $0.02 to $0.03 negative impact from backing out the noncontrolling interest portion of the Elanco profit in the fourth quarter which had not been included in the prior guidance.
Our updated guidance implies fourth quarter non-GAAP EPS of between $1.33 and $1.38, which exceeds current consensus. However, it is lower than our third quarter EPS due to the entry of generic competition for Cialis in the U.S. in late September, higher fourth quarter R&D expenses to support additional late-stage investment including Olumiant and Taltz NILEX as well as and GIP/GLP-1, and launching investments for Emgality and finally the minority interest distributed to Elanco that I mentioned earlier.
On a reported basis, earnings per share for 2018 is now expected to be in the range of $3.13 to $3.18.
In total, we expect strong full-year performance, led by volume gains in our new products, which allows for targeted investments in our long-term portfolio and positions us well to achieve our 2020 financial objectives. This robust performance, along with the capital raised from the Elanco transaction, provide strong cash flow, which really point thoughtfully across our capital allocation priorities. As we move forward in line with our strategy, we will continue to prioritize funding our existing market products, the largest life cycle opportunity and in replenishing our pipeline. In addition, we continue to leverage business development to upgrade our pipeline and future growth prospects as you saw with several recent announcements of business development deals. Finally, we'll return excess cash to shareholders via increases to dividends and share buybacks.
Now I will turn the call back over to Dave to review the pipeline and key future events.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext:
Thanks, Josh. So Slide 18 shows select pipeline opportunities as of October 30. In my summary of the quarter at the beginning of the call, I mentioned a positive for Emgality and lasmiditan, additional movements since our last earnings call includes: the start of a Phase III study for Olumiant in lupus and the start of a Phase II/III adaptive study for Olumiant and alopecia Arriaga. The attrition of Cyramza's second line bladder cancer indication from Phase III and the attrition of the N3pG plus BACE combo arm from the ongoing phase II. Please note we're still studying N3pG as a monotherapy in Phase II.
On Slide 19, we provide an update on expected key events for 2018. In addition to the pipeline movement and regulatory actions I've already noted, you'll see that we've added a new line to reflect our expectations to submit Taltz for axial spondyloarthritis to regulatory authorities by the end of the year. In addition, the FDA has granted breakthrough designation to Emgality for the cluster headache indication. And you'll see that we've added a new line to reflect our plans to submit this indication by year-end.
Under regulatory action, you'll see the approval of Verzenio in Europe and Verzenio in Japan and the approval of Fruquintinib in China under the tradename [indiscernible] 
Under regulatory submissions, you'll see the submission of a new indication for Cyramza in Europe and Japan for second line liver cancer.
In the Phase III data presentations and publications section, we've reflected presentations at EASD of the EASE 2 and 3 studies in type 1 diabetes for Empagliflozin. As well as the CARMELINA CV outcomes study for Trajenta, in collaboration with Boehringer Ingelheim. In addition to the presentations at ACR of the COAST V and COAST W axial spondyloarthritis studies for ixekizumab. And in Phase III top line data disclosures section, in addition to the Phase III read-outs for Trulicity and Ultra Rapid Lispro, which I mentioned earlier, we also had a positive Phase III read-out for flortaucipir, our agent. This was another productive quarter contributing to continued pipeline advancement in 2018.
Before we go to the Q&A session, let me briefly sum up the progress we have made this quarter.
In Q3, we continued our strong operating performance in 2018, delivering 7% revenue growth and 12% volume growth, driven by our newest products. As Josh stated earlier, our pharma volume growth was the strongest of this decade.
We continue to relay significant efficiencies in our cost structure leading to operating margin expansion of over 380 basis points, excluding the impact of foreign exchange on international inventories sold.
We have made excellent progress in our pipeline this quarter as well. The approval and launch of Emgality for migraine patients in the U.S. This is our 10th new product launch since 2014. The FDA submission of lasmiditan to treat migraine, positive Phase III data for Ultra Rapid Lispro, the presentation of exciting Phase II data for tirzepatide in type 2 diabetes, and just yesterday's news, that Trulicity demonstrated superiority in the reduction of major adverse cardiovascular events across a broad range of people with type 2 diabetes in the precedent-setting REWIND trial. We also returned $1.6 billion to shareholders via dividends and share repurchases. We completed the Elanco IPO and bolstered our early phase pipeline with the additional of an oral GLP-1, while completing several other business development deals. 
This concludes our prepared remarks, and I'll turn the call over to the Phil to moderate the Q&A session.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Dave. As in prior calls, we would like to take us many questions as possible during the Q&A session [Operator Instructions] So with that, if you can provide instruction for the Q&A session, and then we're ready for the first caller.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: [Operator Instructions] And first, we'll go to the line of Chris Schott with JPMorgan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: My first one was on REWIND results. I think you referred to them as precedent-setting. I know you're assuring the full data next summer, but any additional color you can provide as we try to think about those results in context of prior GLP-1 CV outcomes studies as you kind of balance with healthier patients versus longer follow-up in that study? My second question, you touched a little bit on that in the prepared remarks, but the diabetes portfolio in 3Q, I think we had a few products coming in a bit below expectations after some very strong first half results. Can you just provide any color was there any kind of one-time issues thinking about Humalog or Basaglar that affected this quarter's results?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you for the questions, Chris. Enrique, those are both for you.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Very good. REWIND is indeed a precedent-setting trial. It included the majority of patients without -- with type 2 diabetes without cardiovascular disease. It also had the longest median follow-up over 5 years a low A1c baseline of 7.3. I think we can share this stage really what's on the press release. We will not be sharing more information on REWIND until are intended presentation at ADA next year. We do intend to submit in 2019 for a new indication. Related to Humalog and Basaglar, the overall diabetes portfolio first, I think we're very pleased with the overall volume growth when it comes to diabetes portfolio. Clearly, when it comes to Humalog, we did see a 14% price decline vis-à-vis Q3 of 2017. Just to provide a little bit more color on this, we are seeing an unfavorable segment mix, about 8 points of that 14. Patient affordability is impacting the insulin portfolio, that's another 4 points. And then we have unfavorable adjustment as a comparator to last year's quarter. Maybe to provide and try to be a little bit more instructive, we do see a lot of volatility with Humalog. It is better to look at this product than a year-to-date basis. And when we look at it on a year-to-date basis, I think, the trend that we basically see is mid-single-digit erosion on price. Then Basaglar are slightly different in that Basaglar didn't have any access in Part D and the fact that we first gained this year. So when we look at the compare, we are comparing on a segment that is highly rebated, and that basically has an impact on price. Now when we look at Q2 of '18 versus Q3 of '18 for Basaglar, there is some price erosion that net you see year-on-year.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Enrique, thank you for the answers.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Will go to the line of Tim Anderson with Wolfe Research.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: A couple of questions. 2019, can you talk about tailwinds and headwinds? I know you won't be giving official guidance until December, but hoping there are some early trends both on the revenue and spending side that you can maybe call out ahead of that official guidance. So for example, obviously, Cialis will be LOE. If I think about R&D spend, you're going to be starting 8 Phase III trials on your GIP/GLP-1 program, so any color on '19 will be appreciated. And then a second question on insulin category. So at some point, FDA may deem similar insulins as interchangeable or substitutable with brand. Do you think this is more likely than not to occur? And could that be something that happens as soon as 2019? And if not 2019, when?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great, Tim, good to hear your voice again. Welcome back. Josh, if you'd answer the first question on the '19 tailwinds and headwinds. And then Enrique, the question Tim had on interchangeability for insulin. Josh?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: I think the main tailwinds, or the headwinds that we'll see is the Cialis overhang in 2019 as we will experience the main erosion here in the fourth quarter in the U.S. So that certainly will be a headwind as we head into 2019. With that being said, we are seeing great growth in our -- the 10 new medicines we've launched since 2014. So we'd expect that those -- the volume from those products to more than compensate from any of the tailwinds we've said we'll see. So we will see a difficult compare. The other thing that we talked about in terms of headwind on the top line would be the change in the donut hole provision in Medicare Part D, the move from 50% to 70%, as Enrique mentioned, we got Basaglar in our diabetes products are in that segment strongly, so we'll see a little bit of headwind there, but I think we're really pleased with the volume growth that we saw this quarter and expect that the main components of that will continue into 2019. On the R&D side, we do have a very robust set of Phase III opportunities ahead of us. You mentioned, which will be starting in 2019, in a broad sense we've got mirikizumab that we moved into Phase III and those trials are expensive so we'll have costs are there. We'll see data on our ARMO asset IL-10 in 2019. We would expect it given positive data there that continue to push that program. So we'll make targeted investments in R&D and should expect to see some growth there. I think we've been pretty clear on the SG&A line that a big component of our operating margin expansion will be to redirect the investments in resources that we have in products like Cialis and Forteo and others. Two are new launch opportunities we'll have -- certainly, we'll spend competitively to launch product like Emgality next year, but we look to manage that total base at a pretty flat level. All in all, we are excited about the prospects for midterm 2019 and 2020. We are well on track, we may meet our 2020 midterm objectives which are 5% compound annual -- minimum 5% compound annual sales growth between 2015 and 2020, and operating margin, a minimum of 18%, so we feel good about where we are there.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Josh. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: When it comes to insulin interchangeability, at the pharmacy level, we do not believe this is imminent. At best, it's likely 50 years away and it will require the appropriate studies for the FDA to provide such information.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Next, we'll go to the line of Jason Gerberry with Bank of America. And next we'll go to the line of Andrew Baum with Citi.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: A couple of questions to Enrique, please. Can you talk to the barriers prohibiting broader Jardiance usage, whether it's education, the investment co-pay? And then related to that, could you outline the Tier 2 formulary stages within Medicare for Jardiance, especially? And in particular, whether a second brand at a net price rather than the current list price entails an increase in penetration by reducing the burden on the patients and preparing grounds for world net pricing which seem to be potentially in products many times.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Andrew, thank you for the questions. If I got them, first, Enrique, would be barriers, you see the Jardiance usage and ways maybe to change that, to formulary status in Medicare an opportunity for second brand to potentially address some of the issues that patients face in that book of business.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Yes, I'm going to try to answer that question, Andrew, just a bit of color on how we see Jardiance because I do see there are some barriers, but I see a number of dynamics that are very positive when I look at the overall class, and Jardiance in particular. First, clearly, one of the things that we look at is how is the overall class evolving and developing. And the class right now is growing in single digits, high single digits, but more importantly, we are seeing new patient starts, the growth in new patient starts accelerate. We now see that in the 14%, 15% range. It reminds me a bit of the reacceleration of the GLP-1 class back when Trulicity was launched. Importantly, there are new guidelines that have been published by both EVA and EASD. We think those guidelines are significant improvements, and that's going to be very helpful. That's an important tailwind. And finally, we have improved access for Jardiance. As you know, we were excluded from one of the major accounts. We are back as of January 1 and that's going to be very important. Access for Jardiance when it comes to coverage now starting in January of '19 is in the 90s, so that's excellent foundation for us to capitalize on. And an additional point which I emphasize in the year U2 1 class, but it is relevant for now as [indiscernible] is that we have basically additional data from competitors that are confirming the benefits when it comes to CV of the class, and importantly, investments from those competitors. Jardiance is in the best opportunity to capitalize on that. So I see an evolution. There are a lot of signs, dynamics to be hopeful about the acceleration of the overall growth and the ability for Jardiance to capitalize off that. You asked about Medicare and whether we would be launching a second brand. Honestly, that's not where I see the issue for Jardiance when it comes to the use of it. Clearly, we always assess all opportunities before our brands, but we are much more focused about, I think as you implied on your question, continuing to educate professionals to ensure that they understand the full benefit that Jardiance can offer patients come and these new guidelines are going to be extremely helpful to do that.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Next we'll go to the line of Jason Gerberry with Bank of America.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: First question is just on Taltz. Can you talk about the outlook going into the next few years with a new competitor launching? And do you believe that [indiscernible] market position with Humira, which has been a leading product in some of these market, do you think that confers any advantage as it pertains to access relative to the already on market interleukin agents? And then my second question, on the oral GLP-1, can you talk a little about what you hope to accomplish here, especially with great data on GIP/GLP? And as we think about novos oral sema, there are obviously some challenges with that product in terms of those things are, so can you talk about sort of what you're looking for in terms of goal no go profile?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great Jason, thank you for the question. So Christi, if you'll take the question on the Taltz outlook going forward. And then over to you Dan for our goals for the oral GLP-1 program.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Sure. So we're very excited about Taltz. I mean, you probably saw that our year-over-year volume growth was significant, specifically our GRx grew 80% year-over-year and over the quarter, continuing to grow the same and we're just starting with Taltz. I have great position in the dermatology office. We just launched this year in the rheumatology office with psoriatic arthritis which that launch is going well and it's coming off of the American College of Rheumatology, we also demonstrated our data in ankylosing spondylitis which were significant before the end of the year, as Dave said. So as we're looking and winning in dermatology, we are beefing up in rheumatology as well. And to your point about access, right now, our access is about the same as [indiscernible] a little bit less due to the optimum change at the beginning of the year. But with that, as you could see the growth of Taltz, it hasn't prevented us from getting patients who need help on Taltz, and we see that continuing in the future because in this marketplace, unfortunately, patients cycle through many different therapies. And what we are seeing from customers is that they would like a variety of therapies with different mechanisms to choose from so you look at the entire portfolio that we have of newer agents coming out in IL-17 and IL-23, our JACK inhibitor, we're looking being able to provide a broad portfolio to those customers, those patients and broaden our access to payers with the advent of the newer agents.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thanks, Christi. Dan?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you for the question on partnership with Chugai on this oral GLP-1 receptor agonist. This is still a pretty Phase 1 molecule, but moving quickly towards human trials. The important comment there is this is a nonpeptic agonist. So this is -- you should think of this very differently from an oral peptide. This is a small molecule approach. As such, we expect it to have in humans as we would all be testing a much higher bioavailability than you would see for any kind of oral peptide approach. What does that mean for patients? We hope that will translate into a better experience, certainly, in terms of how and when this molecule will be dosed for the people on the trials, and ultimately in practice, but also that you translate into improved outcomes. So still very early but we're excited about the potential here and looking forward to getting you updated.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Dan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: And of course, the Phase II that we saw last month, any expectations there on enrollment timelines? And then second question is just another on REWIND. Just curious on what your view is on the incremental opportunity here. Any thoughts on how it might change in the treatment paradigm? And what the impact on overall GLP-1 class might be?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Second quarter for the question. So Enrique, we will have you attack those. So the first one on GIP/GLP or tirzepatide, things are being done with Phase III with titration, things we learned in our phase II program and comments on enrollment timing and then how you see REWIND playing out going forward to GLP-1 market.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: So as we've said as part of tirzepatide's Phase III program, we are planning to study 3 maintenance doses, 5, 10 and 15 milligrams. I think what we've learned is that we should titrate in smaller increments and over time. As we've said, we have a titration study that we intend to share details next year, but clearly we have learned a lot from that study in terms of what are the optimum titration schedules. We are planning aggressively when it comes to start of this trial and starting enrollment. So you'll be hearing more when it comes to our trial for type 2 diabetes soon. As far as REWIND, I don't want to speculate on that particular indication that we will get, but clearly, this is important for the class and that it confirms what other GLP-1s have shown when it comes to seeing benefit. I'm sorry, I cannot resist but I heard the Novartis call and they also mentioned that GLP-1s were not all the same. I think we agree with them.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: And next, we'll go to the line of Steve Scala with Cohen.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: I have a couple of questions. You mentioned the U.S. price hit from gaining access for Basaglar, Taltz and Humalog, but is there one-time volume gain from this reflected in the 17% U.S. volume increase? So that's the first question. The second question is to go back to the questions on REWIND. Is REWIND precedent-setting because of the population studied? Or is it precedent-setting because you have avoided the less-than-optimal outcomes of Novo's leader in SUSTAIN 6? Or perhaps both? And the precedent-setting is a big statement, so in the absence of any color, I guess, we have to assume you hit all 3 components of the primary endpoint, which is something Novo was not able to accomplish?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great, Steve. Thank you for the questions. So Josh, if you'll take the comment on the U.S. price and also seeming offsets either in the volumes we are gaining. And then Enrique, on what we mean by precedent-setting for REWIND?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Steve. Yes, if you look at volume for the third quarter in U.S., it did grew 17% which Dave and I both mentioned, was more significant volume growth we've seen this decade. And that Basaglar absolutely contributed to that growth. So the access that we've got in Medicare Part D is contributing to the 17% growth as are the other elements that we mentioned around patient access and all of the new launches. So it is a positive offset there. You can see in total, even with that negative 6% growth, U.S. business grew 11% overall.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Okay, thanks, Josh. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: As I mentioned, when I first discussed REWIND as part of the first question, it is precedent-setting but because it includes some enrollment of patients without cardiovascular disease. It has the longest median follow-up and a low A1c baseline. We'll be sharing the detailed results at the ADA next year.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Next, we'll go to the line of Eumer Raffat with Evercore.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: First, maybe on oncology side. I noticed for your IL-10, you have decent monotherapy activity in the renal cell setting, but the randomized trials you're running are long and pancreatic, but just wanted to get the thought process there. Is there a consideration? And also your confidence on those open labeled lung trial that is coming off on first half '19. Second, on NGF, it seems like the placebo adjusted efficacy definitely stated into the latest readout? And my question is, do you look at this current placebo adjusted efficacy from this latest trial as being pretty meaningful? And also just your thoughts on the joint replacements on that trial.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great, Umer. Thank you for the questions. So Dan, we'll go to you for those on the development strategy for the IL-10, and then also what we think about the efficacy results and any safety findings from the initial trial read out for NGF.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thanks, Umer for those good questions. So starting with the pegilodecakin IL-10. As you point out, the randomized trial's ongoing Phase III is in pancreas. We're excited about that and look forward to seeing data there in combination with Paul Fox. The 2 studies that you mentioned a randomized trials in non-small cell lung cancer. The first one is in the first line setting in PD-L1 high patients and the second one is in the second line setting in the PD-L1 low setting, both in combination with IO. We are absolutely excited to see data from those trials starting next year, which will inform our progress in that opportunity. As for RCC, you're right that we have the monotherapy activity there in that early Phase I study, which was exciting and part of the basis of the back here is that this molecule is active in the monotherapy setting. I think in terms of that indication, other indications, we still working through what our plan is. Some of that depend on the data that will get in these next couple of trials as well as the data that we already have. We hope to be able to share more with you about our plans for that molecule at our December R&D Day. With respect to tanezumab, I think you had a question there on both the efficacy and the safety with respect to joint replacements. On efficacy, it's hard to make cost trial comparisons. Of course, this is a different population, so it's more treatment resistant, more severe population than in those prior studies. Still, on balance, the efficacy looks quite comparable to what we would expect, maybe some small differences in placebo group from trial to trial. This is a small trial and we look forward to the full results coming. But all in all, I would see the efficacy data are where we expect them to be. And we're really, I think, excited, as we said before about the potential of this molecule from an efficacy perspective. The question, of course, is can we adequately discharge the safety risk? So the total joint replacement, there was an imbalance in this small study, we don't fully understand what drove that imbalance. I think there were a number of factors that we noticed about the imbalance in total joint replacements. One was that most of these events occurred in the follow-up period rather than on treatment period. Another observation is that these events were generally in the patients who are most severe at enrollment. It was in their index joints and it was not, in general, in most cases, it was not associated with any adverse event or any finding in the joint adjudication. So we'll keep our eye in that. And of course, the big readout for us with our partners, Pfizer, would be next year when we get that large 56-week study.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Dan.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Certainly. Next is Alex Arfaei with BMO capital.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: First, on REWIND. Enrique, 2019 seems like a long time to wait for the result. You mentioned you plan to file indication for 2019, so when should we expect to see it in the Trulicity label? And then I have a couple of questions on Verzenio, please. The market seems to have slowed down more than we expected. Just wondering if you could give us your thoughts there. You are gaining share, which is great, but what's going on with the overall market? And we noticed you moved up the timeline for the Adjuvant study by 1 year on clinical trial. Just wondering if you changed anything about the trial? Or is it just your expectations about based on the powering of the study or expected benefit?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you for the questions, Alex. So timing for a potential label update on REWIND, Enrique, for you? And then Ann, if you'd like to handle what we're seeing for market growth with CDK4/6 class and change in timing on clinicaltrials.gov for the adjuvant study. Enrique?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Well, clearly, we're planning to pursue an indication under a submission in 2019 and standard review. We will be looking at a label update in 2020. Of course, we are working as fast as we can to try to expedite that as much as possible. I will be working with the regulatory authorities. Unfortunately, we won't be able to share the REWIND data prior to ADA.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Enrique. Ann?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thanks for the question on Verzenio. We have seen since living on the growth of CDK4/6 market as others in the class have commented. However, we do still see potential growth as more physicians trial this class, particularly as they trial Verzenio. As you mentioned, our NBRx rate is in the high teens, and we are continued to be pleased with this growth. And we're encouraged by a couple of specific factors. So first, as we continue to drive trials with Verzenio in the U.S., what we're seeing is a win position, trial Verzenio, they have a really good experience and likely to prescribe again, so becoming adopters. What seems to particularly resonate with physicians is the fact that patients concerning clinical characteristics that indicate a poor prognosis to do well on Verzenio. So these are patients with liver metastases, high tumor grade, disease or cancer that's not confined at the bone. And so we'll be publishing that additional data shortly. We're also very enthusiastic about the fact that we received approval in Japan and in Europe, so we see those market as significant growth drivers. And then third, related to your question around Adjuvant, we do see potential opportunity in the broader breast cancer market following read-outs in the HER2-positive population, our OS readout from the MONARCH 2 study. and then very importantly, the wreck, she said in Adjuvant breast cancer. On Adjuvant specifically, what we're seeing is that the study is enrolling very well. It's more than 60% enrolled, so we're quite encouraged by that timeline. So we do expect results in 2021 and we look forward to that market because as you know, Adjuvant is really twice the size of the metastatic breast cancer patients so a significant growth opportunity for Lilly in the future.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Ann.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Next will be David Risinger with Morgan Stanley.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: So Lilly's volume growth exact growth has been set to continue to be very strong. But could you please comment on 3 quick questions on pricing. First, the list price outlook for 2019 that you have on your planning assumptions. Second, your exposure to a step up in the donut hole fill in 2019. And third, your exposure to growth in copay accumulators in 2019.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great, Dave. Thank you for the questions. Josh, if you'd like to answer those questions on expectations moving forward on price.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: So Dave, we'll do our guidance for 2019 in December and we'll provide our pricing assumption. Then I can tell you for 2018, for the guidance that we gave, we're not assuming any additional price increases this year. In terms of exposure to the change in the donut hole provision, we've said before that we look across our portfolio. It's about $200 million of incremental impact in 2019. And then on the copay accumulators, of course, these impact specialty drugs, particularly Taltz and Forteo for now, at least for where we are right now, we're comfortable there is not a significant exposure here.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Just let maybe a follow-on comment on that. I have seen notes as well as other industry commentators said that roughly, and especially segments, these are single-digit impacts in terms of patients actually exposed to the out-of-pocket cost of copay accumulators, net of industry actions and payer actions, and that's not really different for us with Taltz, which is the major product affected. So it's an issue, but it's not a huge driver of the overall picture.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thanks, Dave.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: And it will be Vamil Divan with Crédit Suisse.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: So one, just on Olumiant. Looks like still pretty good European uptake, but sort of limited uptake in the first few months of the market in the U.S. so maybe you can just talk about your access in your initial physician interest in that product. And then the other one is maybe a little bit of a different product, we don't talk much about is Lartruvo, so one of your indications but I think it's asked about a little less. I just want to get a better sense of that market. I mean, the growth is still there but just slowing a little bit I'm just trying to get a better sense of how penetrated you think that product is into the market and maybe a little bit more in the longer-term potential for that one?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great, Vamil. Thank you for the question. So Christi, if you'd like to talk about what we're seeing in terms of uptake access and clinician experience here in the U.S. And then, Ann over to you for what we're seeing Lartruvo and the market opportunity there.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Sure. So thank you for the questions, Vamil. In terms of Olumiant, I will start with the EU, as you suggested or outside of the U.S. The growth of Olumiant in volume in the Europe in the last 2 quarters is actually been the fastest growing in volume for Lilly as a whole. And in Japan, we just -- we're lifted in September on the 2-week prescription, so we seeing significant uptake now that, that restriction is off. And in the U.S., we are getting a lot of usage in terms of the uptake. We are giving samples in the office to physician so you won't see that in the uptake. But more importantly, what we're hearing from physicians is that their patients are having really positive feedback on our speed of onset just like we saw in Europe and the other 50 countries where we've launched, the efficacy continues to deliver and really helping patients with that early onset of symptom relief so they can get back to the productive days of life which is significant. As physicians see those patients come back, it truly is changing and then helps them, obviously, feel more comfortable in prescribing as well. And Olumiant is really key for us. It's not just in the U.S. RA but we continue to evolve the indications for Olumiant. We have 3 Phase III studies, obviously, that we are putting forward, atopic dermatitis, in lupus and a Phase II/III adaptive study in alopecia areata. So as those readouts come out as well, we'll be looking for significant uptake of Olumiant in helping a lot of patients with high unmet need.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Thank you, Christi. Ann?

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: So we are pleased with the uptake on Lartruvo and it has been steadily increasing since launch. So as you saw the year-to-date, so we've had a 54% increase over last year and just a 41% increase in this quarter. And Lartruvo is the market leader now in the U.S. in first-line metastatic soft tissue sarcoma, it has about a 20% market share. And then we were excited to see that it is now the market leader in patient, passing this year at a 36% share of market. And as you know well, it remains the only medicine in 4 decades that actually helps patients live longer when compared to dox alone. So we're also encouraged by the fact that we are you seeing thought leaders clearly refer to it as the standard of care and growth in the topic particularly is a very strong. In fact, 97% of the top 100 institutions have now posted sales since launch of Lartruvo. We are still seeing launch in Europe and still gaining access, so we still think there's potential for growth obviously for the market. Our major focus really is on driving awareness in the community and in the centers of excellence where a lot of these key treatment decisions are made. So we do feel, still see quite a bit of potential for growth for Lartruvo, but I really acknowledge it's an orphan disease population, we see penetration but we don't see that yet. We still see a good potential growth and are pleased on how we're doing.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: Great. Thank you, Ann.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: It would be Louise Chen with Cantor Fitzgerald.

headline: Eli Lilly and Company, Q3 2018 Earnings Call, Nov 06, 2018
componenttext: So my first question is on REWIND. Has this met your internal expectation for relative risk reduction? And then secondly, on the injectables CGRPs, how do you see this market landscape playing out with 3 approved products and more to come on the injectable and oral side?
